cataloging_subject.label: Speculum Guy de Warwick
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous early-fourteenth-century Middle English didactic poem or verse homily now generally called Speculum Guy de Warwick. It uses Guy of Warwick asking Alcuin for spiritual instruction as a brief narrative frame around teaching on virtues, vices, salvation, penance, humility, death, and almsgiving. It excludes the Guy of Warwick romance tradition, Alcuin's Latin Liber de virtutibus et vitiis, the ten manuscript codices and their adjacent works, Morrill's 1898 edition and apparatus, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and digital facsimiles as distinct layers.

catalog_summary.title: Speculum Guy de Warwick
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Probably composed in the early fourteenth century; attested in early-fourteenth-century and circa 1330–1340 witnesses; independently printed in 1896, with the immediate completed edition published in 1898
catalog_summary.region: Middle English work of English cultural origin; exact place of composition is unknown; Morrill's Southern-Midland dialect localization is a historical hypothesis

assertion.notes: This conventional modern title is not attributed to the anonymous author and does not imply identity with a Guy of Warwick romance.

titles.value: Speculum Gydonis de Warewyke secundum Alquinum heremitam

assertion.notes: The colophon associates the sermon with Alcuin as its speaking authority; it is not accepted as an authorship attribution to the historical Alcuin.

titles.value: Amen. Amen. So mot it be.

assertion.notes: This is Morrill's complete reading-text explicit at numbered line 1034, supplied from D because the corresponding A1 leaf portion is lost.

agents.name: Georgiana Lea Morrill
notes: Editor and collator of the immediate 1898 source edition; not the medieval author.

assertion.notes: The work is anonymous. The fictional or legendary framing of Alcuin as Guy's teacher and later manuscript colophons do not establish authorship by the historical Alcuin; neither is John Lydgate responsible for this poem.

notes: Morrill chose A1 as base, supplied damaged matter, and imposed punctuation, paragraphing, capitalization, lineation, bracket conventions, marginal abstracts, and other edition-layer interventions.
Morrill collated A2, D, H1, H2, and R against A1 but generally omitted merely orthographic or phonetic variants.

date_statements.display: Probably composed in the early fourteenth century

assertion.notes: The normalized range operationalizes Edwards's probable early-fourteenth-century dating; it is not a dated authorial event. Morrill's broader 1250–1325 discussion is historical scholarship and is not used to widen the preferred modern statement.

date_statements.display: St John's College Cambridge MS 256 (S.30) is dated to the early fourteenth century, circa 1300–1333

assertion.notes: The witness range overlaps the proposed composition period; no exact copying date or priority within the interval is claimed. The Auchinleck witness provides firmer attestation by circa 1330–1340.

date_statements.display: Carl Horstmann and Georgiana Lea Morrill independently printed texts in 1896; exact publication priority is unresolved

assertion.notes: Morrill says her text was printed in May 1896, while Horstmann's Royal-based text appeared in the 1896 Yorkshire Writers volume. Year-only evidence does not establish which was first, so Morrill's title-page priority claim is not repeated as settled fact.

date_statements.display: Morrill's completed EETS edition was published in 1898

assertion.notes: This statement identifies the immediate complete edition used by U-M, not the earliest printing claim.

places.label: Southern Midlands, England
notes: Morrill's historical localization was intermediate between eastern and western Southern Midland and leaned eastward; it is retained only as an attributed dialect hypothesis.

places.label: Zarephath
notes: Biblical Sarepta/Zarephath, setting of the final Elijah and widow exemplum.

assertion.notes: This is Morrill's localization of the edited textual language, not a securely established place of composition. Current witness dialect distributions cannot be transferred mechanically to the lost authorial text.

scope.description: Guy's legendary identity as earl of Warwick

assertion.notes: Warwick is an association carried by the hero's name, not evidence that the poem was composed there.

scope.description: brief biblical, patristic, and chapter-heading quotations embedded within the Middle English poem

assertion.notes: Brief Latin insertions do not make the work mixed-form or evenly bilingual. Morrill's English marginal abstracts and notes belong to the edition layer.

form_statements.description: A 1,034-line verse homily in short rhyming couplets, with brief embedded Latin quotations.

assertion.notes: The brief Guy/Alcuin exchange frames an extended sermon rather than a sustained romance narrative.

subject_statements.label: Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Almsgiving and care for the poor
Guy of Warwick and Alcuin as devotional authorities

assertion.notes: The legendary figures authorize the sermon. Their narrative association is historically false and is not evidence for authorship or an actual meeting.

subject_statements.label: Elijah and the widow of Zarephath

summaries.value: After announcing instruction for the health of the soul, the poem introduces the penitent Guy of Warwick, who asks Alcuin for a written sermon on how to leave worldly temptation. Alcuin teaches the recognition and practice of virtues, rejection of deadly sin, Christian salvation and judgment, paradise and the Godhead, repentance, confession, prayer, mercy, patience, humility, preparation for death, and almsgiving. The final extended exemplum retells Elijah's encounter with the widow of Zarephath to demonstrate charity's spiritual and material reward.

The fullest form has 1,034 short-couplet lines. Guy's request and the opening virtues/vices teaching occupy roughly lines 1–166; salvation, judgment, paradise, and the Godhead lines 167–399; penance and mercy lines 400–568; patience and humility lines 569–691; avoidance of sin and preparation for death lines 692–918; and almsgiving with the widow of Zarephath exemplum lines 919–1034.

The work belongs to the non-romance devotional reception of Guy of Warwick. It is anonymous and is not Alcuin's work: current scholarship finds at most a tenuous relationship between approximately the first 137 lines and Alcuin's Liber de virtutibus et vitiis, with the remainder a free English redaction or independent compilation. Morrill's marginal summaries, Latin chapter headings aligned to Alcuin, punctuation, lineation, and bracketed supplies are edition-layer features.

extent.description: Current scholarship describes the fullest form as 1,034 short-couplet lines, matching Morrill's final line number. The XML has 1,033 direct poem L elements because numbered line 14 is absent; it also has forty L elements nested inside apparatus NOTE elements. Those forty comprise witness-only interpolations, replacement readings, and a twelve-line Harley 525 alternate ending and are not added to the reading-text extent. The XML further contains 4 DIV1, 45 PB, 305 MILESTONE, 1,377 NOTE, and 24 P elements. The NLS project headnote reports 1,031 lines under a different counting description, so all totals remain explicitly scoped.

content_parts.label: Guy's request and instruction in virtues and vices
content_parts.summary: The speaker calls for attention, introduces the penitent Guy of Warwick and Alcuin, records Guy's request for a sermon, and begins moral instruction with virtues and the deadly sins.

locators.value: opening through MILESTONE N=164 and following couplet

content_parts.label: Salvation, judgment, paradise, and the Godhead
content_parts.summary: Teaching on original sin and redemption, the Last Judgment, paradise, and Trinitarian doctrine.

content_parts.label: Penance, confession, prayer, and mercy
content_parts.summary: Instruction on penitence and confession develops into teaching on prayer and divine and human mercy.

content_parts.label: Patience and humility
content_parts.summary: The sermon commends patience under suffering and humility as central Christian virtues.

content_parts.label: Avoidance of sin and preparation for death
content_parts.summary: Admonitions to flee sin lead to sustained meditation on the uncertainty and imminence of death.

content_parts.label: Almsgiving and the widow of Zarephath
content_parts.summary: The conclusion urges charity, promises its double spiritual and worldly good, and retells Elijah's encounter with the widow whose meal and oil are miraculously sustained.
notes: The A1 ending is physically defective; Morrill completes lines 1007–1034 chiefly from D, with one word at line 1029 from R.

related_works.title: De virtutibus et vitiis liber
notes: Alcuin composed this Latin work around 800 for Count Wido/Guido of Tours. Current scholarship considers a direct relationship to the Speculum tenuous: approximately the first 137 lines have some affinity, but the remainder is a free English redaction or independent moral compilation. The poem is not cataloged as a translation of Alcuin.

related_works.title: Guy of Warwick romance tradition
notes: The Speculum transfers the famous penitent Guy into devotional instruction but does not narrate the romance and is not a redaction of its plot.

related_works.title: 1 Kings 17:8–16: Elijah and the widow of Zarephath
notes: The final extended exemplum freely retells the biblical episode to prove the double good of almsgiving.

related_works.title: John Lydgate's Guy of Warwick
notes: A distinct non-romance devotional use of Guy studied alongside the anonymous Speculum; Lydgate is not the Speculum's author.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00039.xml
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD; IDG; FRONT; BODY; direct and NOTE-nested L elements
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for identifiers, Middle English and Latin content, title, incipit, explicit, narrative and instruction, edition-derived marginalia and apparatus, page and line milestones, and documented 2016 review.

evidence.citation: XPath and structural inspection of CME00039.xml against Morrill's lineation
evidence.locator: DIV1, PB, L, NOTE, MILESTONE, P; direct-work versus NOTE-descendant L counts
evidence.summary: Finds 4 DIV1, 45 PB, 1,073 L, 1,377 NOTE, 305 MILESTONE, and 24 P elements. Of the L elements, 1,033 are direct reading text and forty are apparatus variants, including Harley 525's twelve-line replacement ending; Morrill's numbered line 14 is absent from the direct sequence.

evidence.citation: Georgiana Lea Morrill, ed., Speculum Gy de Warewyke, EETS Extra Series 75 (1898)
evidence.locator: title page; introduction; poem pp. 3–45; notes pp. 47–102
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the immediate edition, titles, authorial anonymity, witness descriptions, edited text, content, lineation, dialect hypothesis, apparatus, and complete-volume structure.

evidence.citation: Morrill's preface to Speculum Gy de Warewyke
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence that Morrill's text was printed in May 1896 before the completed volume appeared in 1898.

evidence.citation: Morrill's editorial method and textual notes
evidence.locator: pp. lviii–lxiv and notes to lines 1007–1034
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for A1 base selection, comparison witnesses, selective variants, D-supplied ending, bracket/parenthesis/italic conventions, punctuation, capitalization, paragraphing, and marginal abstracts.

evidence.citation: A. S. G. Edwards, ‘The Speculum Guy de Warwick and Lydgate's Guy of Warwick’ (2007), pp. 81–93
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for preferred title, anonymous verse-homily genre, probable early-fourteenth-century date, 1,034-line extent, ten-witness census, thematic structure, non-romance Guy reception, and the tenuous Alcuin relationship.

evidence.citation: NLS Auchinleck project, Speculum Guy de Warwick headnote
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the title, short-couplet form, NIMEV 1101, A1 location and mutilated ending, Scribe 2, line-count convention, and ten-witness census correcting Morrill.

evidence.citation: Biblissima record for St John's College Cambridge MS 256 (S.30)
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the early-fourteenth-century witness, its codex context, and the work at pp. 254–268.

evidence.citation: British Library Royal MS 17 B XVII
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the R witness, its colophon, Morrill's edition, and Horstmann's 1896 Royal-based publication.
Companion-lineage evidence for the additional witness titled and closed Alquyne, its date, location, and 971-line extent.

open_questions.question: When and where was the poem composed, and how much of Morrill's Southern-Midland dialect localization remains accepted after comparison of all ten witnesses?
next_steps: Consult current linguistic and textual scholarship across all ten witnesses rather than transferring any codex's origin or scribal dialect to the work.
Keep early-fourteenth-century composition at medium confidence and exact composition place unknown until stronger evidence is found.
Distinguish composition language, exemplar language, and individual witness dialects in any refinement.

open_questions.question: What demonstrable textual relationship, if any, joins the opening of Speculum Guy de Warwick to Alcuin's De virtutibus et vitiis liber?
next_steps: Compare the Middle English opening directly with a critical Latin text of Alcuin and with likely intermediary florilegia or sermon collections.
Separate the poem's fictional Guy/Alcuin encounter and Morrill's editorial Latin headings from verbal source evidence.
Retain related_to rather than translation_of or adaptation_of until the textual dependence is demonstrated.

open_questions.question: Did Horstmann's Royal-based text or Morrill's May 1896 printing appear first?
next_steps: Find dated publisher records, EETS correspondence, reviews, presentation copies, or issue notices for both 1896 printings.
Do not encode Morrill's title-page claim of first publication as settled modern fact until priority is established.

open_questions.question: How should the NLS headnote's 1,031-line count, Morrill's numbered 1,034-line text, the 1,033 XML direct L elements, and forty NOTE-nested variant lines be reconciled?
next_steps: Collate Morrill's printed pp. 3–45 against the XML and identify the status of numbered line 14.
Document whether each published total includes Latin quotations, missing couplets, supplied lines, and alternate witness endings.
Keep all computational counts scoped to their structural XPath and do not use count(//L) as a reading-text extent.

open_questions.question: Can the early-fourteenth-century dating and textual position of St John's MS 256 be narrowed enough to determine whether it predates the Auchinleck witness?
next_steps: Obtain the current institutional description and complete images for codicological and palaeographical review.
Use an updated specialist catalog or direct institutional dating before narrowing the current 1300–1333 range.

notes: Work, manuscript witnesses, scholarly editions, electronic encoding, repository artifact, and facsimiles are separate cataloging layers.
Anonymous authorship is maintained: Alcuin is a narrative authority and possible loose source analogue, not the demonstrated author; John Lydgate wrote a distinct Guy poem.
The four witnesses not used by Morrill—Add MS 36983, Manchester English MS 50, St John's MS 256, and Bodleian Add. C. 220—are related sources rather than inputs to the immediate edition.
Thematic content ranges summarize Edwards's analysis and Morrill's lineation; they are not asserted as authorial section divisions.
